2: A growing concern with the depletion of natural resources has encouraged the search for alternative renewable sources for the current energy demand worldwide (Lima et al., 2020). Countries have fostered the implementation of public policies to encourage the use of non-fossil sources to preserve resources and reduce environmental damage (Deng et al., 2020).

The Brazilian government instituted in April 2021 the "Fuel of the Future Program" to propose measures to increase the use of renewable fuels, besides stimulating the national vehicle technology aiming at the

decarbonization of the national transport energy matrix (Brasil, 2021). These measures follow the country's commitment to the Paris agree- ment, where Brazil committed to reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions by 43% until 2030 (Lima et al., 2020). To meet this target, the country projects an increase in sustainable biofuels participation to approximately 18% in the national energy matrix until 2030 (Brasil, 2015, 2021).

The current need to mitigate environmental impacts and simulta- neously be allied with economic development has changed consumption patterns and production chains and linear systems are no longer sus- tainable (Borges et al., 2021). There is a need to change the thought of

waste at the end of the process, incorporating reuse and recycling op- tions in production chains towards a circular and low carbon economy, adding value to waste (Wainaina et al., 2020). In this context, anaerobic digestion (AD) has been one of the technologies currently employed for energy recovery from waste worldwide (Rekleitis et al., 2020). Biogas from AD can be used for energy purposes in several ways, either by replacing liquefied petroleum gas, cogeneration of electricity and heat, or biofuel (Baena-Moreno et al., 2021).
In Brazil, according to data released by the International Center for Renewable Energy (CIBiogas, 2021), agriculture and livestock represent

the primary sources of substrate used for biogas production, corre- sponding to 79% of the AD plants operating in the country as the pri- mary substrate. Livestock has particular relevance. According to estimates by the United States Department of Agriculture and Foreign Agricultural Service (USDA), in 2021, Brazil should be responsible for 23% of global meat exports, configuring itself as an important branch of the national economy (USDA, 2021).

The management of livestock waste is fundamental in searching for a more sustainable future since this type of activity corresponds to about 15% of global anthropogenic GHG emissions. That is, the sector is

responsible for the emission of 7.1 GT CO2 eq. and this value increases every year due to the increase in food demand driven by population growth, which pressures the sector to expand production, highlighting the importance of the adoption of a low carbon economy (FAO, 2017).

Brazil is the fourth-largest producer of pork globally and relies on a confined animal production system as the primary production strategy to meet market demand (USDA, 2021). However, despite the produc- tivity benefits of this configuration, there is the generation of a high volume of effluents concentrated in a small geographical area, which are known for their polluting potential, the GHG emission, eutrophication of watercourses, and impacts on air quality (Tallaksen et al., 2020). To bypass these damages, AD has been one of the allied strategies of pig farming in managing these effluents, allowing energy recovery and reducing GHG emissions. Animal waste can be an essential substrate for the AD in the search for change in the national energy matrix, making this activity environmentally viable towards a country with low carbon meat production (Velásquez Piñas et al., 2019).

While AD is a promising route to waste management, the environ- mental impacts associated with this activity must be carefully analyzed to ensure its environmental viability (Hollas et al., 2021). In this regard, the life cycle assessment (LCA) methodology has been used to examine the environmental impacts of swine waste treatment. Many researches have been dedicated to studying the environmental impacts of different treatment systems aiming at the adequate management of livestock waste combined with the recovery of energy and nutrients resulting from the AD, reporting the impacts and benefits generated by the adopted configurations (Table 1) (Esteves et al., 2019).

According to Table 1, the configuration adopted for the management of livestock waste can have different roles in the production of biogas and consequently in the LCA. this is reflected in the values of the envi- ronmental impacts reported, which present great variability ranging from kg CO2 eq. to tons of CO2 eq. for the biogas generation scenarios. Esteves et al. (2019) conducted a review on LCA studies of manure biogas production system in a global perspective and concluded that there are several aspects that make the comparison of these results critical, according to the authors biogas production presents many variables that make it difficult to compare the results, the technological level of the plants, the sources of energy and raw material, as well as climatic and regional issues, can affect the evaluation, which justifies this variability found in the literature results.

Although the results are diverse, the use of biogas as an energy source to generate electricity is one of the most widespread uses of the technology, however, some studies have presented the use of biogas as a substitute for natural gas, mainly for use in food cooking (Wang et al., 2018). In addition, the benefits associated with the recovery of nutrients with the use of digestate as biofertilizer are highlighted in these publi- cations, since the effluent resulting from DA, agronomically, represents an important source of nutrients, not only in terms of food security, due to the depletion of natural sources of phosphorus, for example, but also in environmental terms by replacing the production, use and transport of synthetic fertilizers, responsible for the emission of a significant amount of GHG throughout their life cycle (Walling and Vaneeckhaute, 2020). Consequently, due to the substitution of fossil sources for a renewable matrix, in both scenarios, the environmental gains are perceptible, with mitigations in the global warming potential (GWP), which can exceed -200% (Jiang et al., 2021).

Despite a large amount of information on the subject in the literature, most of these studies do not evaluate existing treatment plants con- cerning electricity generation and the potential for biomethane pro- duction, as a substitute for fossil vehicular fuel (gasoline or diesel), in the Brazilian context of pig farming. Furthermore, the different objec- tives, assumptions, and limits of the systems studied make comparison and extrapolating results difficult. Thus, the present study employs the LCA methodology to evaluate the environmental impacts of the treat- ment of swine manure in terms of AD for electricity generation and biomethane use to understand different waste management systems in
Brazil, their environmental effects and potential improvements offered by each alternative.

responsible for the emission of 7.1 GT CO2 eq. and this value increases every year due to the increase in food demand driven by population growth, which pressures the sector to expand production, highlighting the importance of the adoption of a low carbon economy (FAO, 2017).

Brazil is the fourth-largest producer of pork globally and relies on a confined animal production system as the primary production strategy to meet market demand (USDA, 2021). However, despite the produc- tivity benefits of this configuration, there is the generation of a high volume of effluents concentrated in a small geographical area, which are known for their polluting potential, the GHG emission, eutrophication of watercourses, and impacts on air quality (Tallaksen et al., 2020). To bypass these damages, AD has been one of the allied strategies of pig farming in managing these effluents, allowing energy recovery and reducing GHG emissions. Animal waste can be an essential substrate for the AD in the search for change in the national energy matrix, making this activity environmentally viable towards a country with low carbon meat production (Velásquez Piñas et al., 2019).

While AD is a promising route to waste management, the environ- mental impacts associated with this activity must be carefully analyzed to ensure its environmental viability (Hollas et al., 2021). In this regard, the life cycle assessment (LCA) methodology has been used to examine the environmental impacts of swine waste treatment. Many researches have been dedicated to studying the environmental impacts of different treatment systems aiming at the adequate management of livestock waste combined with the recovery of energy and nutrients resulting from the AD, reporting the impacts and benefits generated by the adopted configurations (Table 1) (Esteves et al., 2019).

According to Table 1, the configuration adopted for the management of livestock waste can have different roles in the production of biogas and consequently in the LCA. this is reflected in the values of the envi- ronmental impacts reported, which present great variability ranging from kg CO2 eq. to tons of CO2 eq. for the biogas generation scenarios. Esteves et al. (2019) conducted a review on LCA studies of manure biogas production system in a global perspective and concluded that there are several aspects that make the comparison of these results critical, according to the authors biogas production presents many variables that make it difficult to compare the results, the technological level of the plants, the sources of energy and raw material, as well as climatic and regional issues, can affect the evaluation, which justifies this variability found in the literature results.

Although the results are diverse, the use of biogas as an energy source to generate electricity is one of the most widespread uses of the technology, however, some studies have presented the use of biogas as a substitute for natural gas, mainly for use in food cooking (Wang et al., 2018). In addition, the benefits associated with the recovery of nutrients with the use of digestate as biofertilizer are highlighted in these publi- cations, since the effluent resulting from DA, agronomically, represents an important source of nutrients, not only in terms of food security, due to the depletion of natural sources of phosphorus, for example, but also in environmental terms by replacing the production, use and transport of synthetic fertilizers, responsible for the emission of a significant amount of GHG throughout their life cycle (Walling and Vaneeckhaute, 2020). Consequently, due to the substitution of fossil sources for a renewable matrix, in both scenarios, the environmental gains are perceptible, with mitigations in the global warming potential (GWP), which can exceed -200% (Jiang et al., 2021).

Despite a large amount of information on the subject in the literature, most of these studies do not evaluate existing treatment plants con- cerning electricity generation and the potential for biomethane pro- duction, as a substitute for fossil vehicular fuel (gasoline or diesel), in the Brazilian context of pig farming. Furthermore, the different objec- tives, assumptions, and limits of the systems studied make comparison and extrapolating results difficult. Thus, the present study employs the LCA methodology to evaluate the environmental impacts of the treat- ment of swine manure in terms of AD for electricity generation and biomethane use to understand different waste management systems in
Brazil, their environmental effects and potential improvements offered by each alternative.
5: A growing concern with the depletion of natural resources has encouraged the search for alternative renewable sources for the current energy demand worldwide (Lima et al., 2020). Countries have fostered the implementation of public policies to encourage the use of non-fossil sources to preserve resources and reduce environmental damage (Deng et al., 2020).

The Brazilian government instituted in April 2021 the "Fuel of the Future Program" to propose measures to increase the use of renewable fuels, besides stimulating the national vehicle technology aiming at the

decarbonization of the national transport energy matrix (Brasil, 2021). These measures follow the country's commitment to the Paris agree- ment, where Brazil committed to reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions by 43% until 2030 (Lima et al., 2020). To meet this target, the country projects an increase in sustainable biofuels participation to approximately 18% in the national energy matrix until 2030 (Brasil, 2015, 2021).

The current need to mitigate environmental impacts and simulta- neously be allied with economic development has changed consumption patterns and production chains and linear systems are no longer sus- tainable (Borges et al., 2021). There is a need to change the thought of

waste at the end of the process, incorporating reuse and recycling op- tions in production chains towards a circular and low carbon economy, adding value to waste (Wainaina et al., 2020). In this context, anaerobic digestion (AD) has been one of the technologies currently employed for energy recovery from waste worldwide (Rekleitis et al., 2020). Biogas from AD can be used for energy purposes in several ways, either by replacing liquefied petroleum gas, cogeneration of electricity and heat, or biofuel (Baena-Moreno et al., 2021).
In Brazil, according to data released by the International Center for Renewable Energy (CIBiogas, 2021), agriculture and livestock represent

the primary sources of substrate used for biogas production, corre- sponding to 79% of the AD plants operating in the country as the pri- mary substrate. Livestock has particular relevance. According to estimates by the United States Department of Agriculture and Foreign Agricultural Service (USDA), in 2021, Brazil should be responsible for 23% of global meat exports, configuring itself as an important branch of the national economy (USDA, 2021).

The management of livestock waste is fundamental in searching for a more sustainable future since this type of activity corresponds to about 15% of global anthropogenic GHG emissions. That is, the sector is

In Brazil, according to data released by the International Center for Renewable Energy (CIBiogas, 2021), agriculture and livestock represent

the primary sources of substrate used for biogas production, corre- sponding to 79% of the AD plants operating in the country as the pri- mary substrate. Livestock has particular relevance. According to estimates by the United States Department of Agriculture and Foreign Agricultural Service (USDA), in 2021, Brazil should be responsible for 23% of global meat exports, configuring itself as an important branch of the national economy (USDA, 2021).

responsible for the emission of 7.1 GT CO2 eq. and this value increases every year due to the increase in food demand driven by population growth, which pressures the sector to expand production, highlighting the importance of the adoption of a low carbon economy (FAO, 2017).

Brazil is the fourth-largest producer of pork globally and relies on a confined animal production system as the primary production strategy to meet market demand (USDA, 2021). However, despite the produc- tivity benefits of this configuration, there is the generation of a high volume of effluents concentrated in a small geographical area, which are known for their polluting potential, the GHG emission, eutrophication of watercourses, and impacts on air quality (Tallaksen et al., 2020). To bypass these damages, AD has been one of the allied strategies of pig farming in managing these effluents, allowing energy recovery and reducing GHG emissions. Animal waste can be an essential substrate for the AD in the search for change in the national energy matrix, making this activity environmentally viable towards a country with low carbon meat production (Velásquez Piñas et al., 2019).

While AD is a promising route to waste management, the environ- mental impacts associated with this activity must be carefully analyzed to ensure its environmental viability (Hollas et al., 2021). In this regard, the life cycle assessment (LCA) methodology has been used to examine the environmental impacts of swine waste treatment. Many researches have been dedicated to studying the environmental impacts of different treatment systems aiming at the adequate management of livestock waste combined with the recovery of energy and nutrients resulting from the AD, reporting the impacts and benefits generated by the adopted configurations (Table 1) (Esteves et al., 2019).

According to Table 1, the configuration adopted for the management of livestock waste can have different roles in the production of biogas and consequently in the LCA. this is reflected in the values of the envi- ronmental impacts reported, which present great variability ranging from kg CO2 eq. to tons of CO2 eq. for the biogas generation scenarios. Esteves et al. (2019) conducted a review on LCA studies of manure biogas production system in a global perspective and concluded that there are several aspects that make the comparison of these results critical, according to the authors biogas production presents many variables that make it difficult to compare the results, the technological level of the plants, the sources of energy and raw material, as well as climatic and regional issues, can affect the evaluation, which justifies this variability found in the literature results.

Although the results are diverse, the use of biogas as an energy source to generate electricity is one of the most widespread uses of the technology, however, some studies have presented the use of biogas as a substitute for natural gas, mainly for use in food cooking (Wang et al., 2018). In addition, the benefits associated with the recovery of nutrients with the use of digestate as biofertilizer are highlighted in these publi- cations, since the effluent resulting from DA, agronomically, represents an important source of nutrients, not only in terms of food security, due to the depletion of natural sources of phosphorus, for example, but also in environmental terms by replacing the production, use and transport of synthetic fertilizers, responsible for the emission of a significant amount of GHG throughout their life cycle (Walling and Vaneeckhaute, 2020). Consequently, due to the substitution of fossil sources for a renewable matrix, in both scenarios, the environmental gains are perceptible, with mitigations in the global warming potential (GWP), which can exceed -200% (Jiang et al., 2021).

Despite a large amount of information on the subject in the literature, most of these studies do not evaluate existing treatment plants con- cerning electricity generation and the potential for biomethane pro- duction, as a substitute for fossil vehicular fuel (gasoline or diesel), in the Brazilian context of pig farming. Furthermore, the different objec- tives, assumptions, and limits of the systems studied make comparison and extrapolating results difficult. Thus, the present study employs the LCA methodology to evaluate the environmental impacts of the treat- ment of swine manure in terms of AD for electricity generation and biomethane use to understand different waste management systems in
Brazil, their environmental effects and potential improvements offered by each alternative.
8: A growing concern with the depletion of natural resources has encouraged the search for alternative renewable sources for the current energy demand worldwide (Lima et al., 2020). Countries have fostered the implementation of public policies to encourage the use of non-fossil sources to preserve resources and reduce environmental damage (Deng et al., 2020).

The Brazilian government instituted in April 2021 the "Fuel of the Future Program" to propose measures to increase the use of renewable fuels, besides stimulating the national vehicle technology aiming at the

decarbonization of the national transport energy matrix (Brasil, 2021). These measures follow the country's commitment to the Paris agree- ment, where Brazil committed to reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions by 43% until 2030 (Lima et al., 2020). To meet this target, the country projects an increase in sustainable biofuels participation to approximately 18% in the national energy matrix until 2030 (Brasil, 2015, 2021).

The current need to mitigate environmental impacts and simulta- neously be allied with economic development has changed consumption patterns and production chains and linear systems are no longer sus- tainable (Borges et al., 2021). There is a need to change the thought of

waste at the end of the process, incorporating reuse and recycling op- tions in production chains towards a circular and low carbon economy, adding value to waste (Wainaina et al., 2020). In this context, anaerobic digestion (AD) has been one of the technologies currently employed for energy recovery from waste worldwide (Rekleitis et al., 2020). Biogas from AD can be used for energy purposes in several ways, either by replacing liquefied petroleum gas, cogeneration of electricity and heat, or biofuel (Baena-Moreno et al., 2021).
In Brazil, according to data released by the International Center for Renewable Energy (CIBiogas, 2021), agriculture and livestock represent

the primary sources of substrate used for biogas production, corre- sponding to 79% of the AD plants operating in the country as the pri- mary substrate. Livestock has particular relevance. According to estimates by the United States Department of Agriculture and Foreign Agricultural Service (USDA), in 2021, Brazil should be responsible for 23% of global meat exports, configuring itself as an important branch of the national economy (USDA, 2021).

Thus, the present study employs the LCA methodology to evaluate the environmental impacts of the treat- ment of swine manure in terms of AD for electricity generation and biomethane use to understand different waste management systems in
Brazil, their environmental effects and potential improvements offered by each alternative.
10: A growing concern with the depletion of natural resources has encouraged the search for alternative renewable sources for the current energy demand worldwide (Lima et al., 2020). Countries have fostered the implementation of public policies to encourage the use of non-fossil sources to preserve resources and reduce environmental damage (Deng et al., 2020).

The Brazilian government instituted in April 2021 the "Fuel of the Future Program" to propose measures to increase the use of renewable fuels, besides stimulating the national vehicle technology aiming at the

decarbonization of the national transport energy matrix (Brasil, 2021). These measures follow the country's commitment to the Paris agree- ment, where Brazil committed to reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions by 43% until 2030 (Lima et al., 2020). To meet this target, the country projects an increase in sustainable biofuels participation to approximately 18% in the national energy matrix until 2030 (Brasil, 2015, 2021).

The current need to mitigate environmental impacts and simulta- neously be allied with economic development has changed consumption patterns and production chains and linear systems are no longer sus- tainable (Borges et al., 2021). There is a need to change the thought of

waste at the end of the process, incorporating reuse and recycling op- tions in production chains towards a circular and low carbon economy, adding value to waste (Wainaina et al., 2020). In this context, anaerobic digestion (AD) has been one of the technologies currently employed for energy recovery from waste worldwide (Rekleitis et al., 2020). Biogas from AD can be used for energy purposes in several ways, either by replacing liquefied petroleum gas, cogeneration of electricity and heat, or biofuel (Baena-Moreno et al., 2021).
In Brazil, according to data released by the International Center for Renewable Energy (CIBiogas, 2021), agriculture and livestock represent

the primary sources of substrate used for biogas production, corre- sponding to 79% of the AD plants operating in the country as the pri- mary substrate. Livestock has particular relevance. According to estimates by the United States Department of Agriculture and Foreign Agricultural Service (USDA), in 2021, Brazil should be responsible for 23% of global meat exports, configuring itself as an important branch of the national economy (USDA, 2021).

The management of livestock waste is fundamental in searching for a more sustainable future since this type of activity corresponds to about 15% of global anthropogenic GHG emissions. That is, the sector is

responsible for the emission of 7.1 GT CO2 eq. and this value increases every year due to the increase in food demand driven by population growth, which pressures the sector to expand production, highlighting the importance of the adoption of a low carbon economy (FAO, 2017).

Brazil is the fourth-largest producer of pork globally and relies on a confined animal production system as the primary production strategy to meet market demand (USDA, 2021). However, despite the produc- tivity benefits of this configuration, there is the generation of a high volume of effluents concentrated in a small geographical area, which are known for their polluting potential, the GHG emission, eutrophication of watercourses, and impacts on air quality (Tallaksen et al., 2020). To bypass these damages, AD has been one of the allied strategies of pig farming in managing these effluents, allowing energy recovery and reducing GHG emissions. Animal waste can be an essential substrate for the AD in the search for change in the national energy matrix, making this activity environmentally viable towards a country with low carbon meat production (Velásquez Piñas et al., 2019).

While AD is a promising route to waste management, the environ- mental impacts associated with this activity must be carefully analyzed to ensure its environmental viability (Hollas et al., 2021). In this regard, the life cycle assessment (LCA) methodology has been used to examine the environmental impacts of swine waste treatment. Many researches have been dedicated to studying the environmental impacts of different treatment systems aiming at the adequate management of livestock waste combined with the recovery of energy and nutrients resulting from the AD, reporting the impacts and benefits generated by the adopted configurations (Table 1) (Esteves et al., 2019).

According to Table 1, the configuration adopted for the management of livestock waste can have different roles in the production of biogas and consequently in the LCA. this is reflected in the values of the envi- ronmental impacts reported, which present great variability ranging from kg CO2 eq. to tons of CO2 eq. for the biogas generation scenarios. Esteves et al. (2019) conducted a review on LCA studies of manure biogas production system in a global perspective and concluded that there are several aspects that make the comparison of these results critical, according to the authors biogas production presents many variables that make it difficult to compare the results, the technological level of the plants, the sources of energy and raw material, as well as climatic and regional issues, can affect the evaluation, which justifies this variability found in the literature results.

Although the results are diverse, the use of biogas as an energy source to generate electricity is one of the most widespread uses of the technology, however, some studies have presented the use of biogas as a substitute for natural gas, mainly for use in food cooking (Wang et al., 2018). In addition, the benefits associated with the recovery of nutrients with the use of digestate as biofertilizer are highlighted in these publi- cations, since the effluent resulting from DA, agronomically, represents an important source of nutrients, not only in terms of food security, due to the depletion of natural sources of phosphorus, for example, but also in environmental terms by replacing the production, use and transport of synthetic fertilizers, responsible for the emission of a significant amount of GHG throughout their life cycle (Walling and Vaneeckhaute, 2020). Consequently, due to the substitution of fossil sources for a renewable matrix, in both scenarios, the environmental gains are perceptible, with mitigations in the global warming potential (GWP), which can exceed -200% (Jiang et al., 2021).

Despite a large amount of information on the subject in the literature, most of these studies do not evaluate existing treatment plants con- cerning electricity generation and the potential for biomethane pro- duction, as a substitute for fossil vehicular fuel (gasoline or diesel), in the Brazilian context of pig farming. Furthermore, the different objec- tives, assumptions, and limits of the systems studied make comparison and extrapolating results difficult. Thus, the present study employs the LCA methodology to evaluate the environmental impacts of the treat- ment of swine manure in terms of AD for electricity generation and biomethane use to understand different waste management systems in
Brazil, their environmental effects and potential improvements offered by each alternative.

(2019) conducted a review on LCA studies of manure biogas production system in a global perspective and concluded that there are several aspects that make the comparison of these results critical, according to the authors biogas production presents many variables that make it difficult to compare the results, the technological level of the plants, the sources of energy and raw material, as well as climatic and regional issues, can affect the evaluation, which justifies this variability found in the literature results.

Although the results are diverse, the use of biogas as an energy source to generate electricity is one of the most widespread uses of the technology, however, some studies have presented the use of biogas as a substitute for natural gas, mainly for use in food cooking (Wang et al., 2018). In addition, the benefits associated with the recovery of nutrients with the use of digestate as biofertilizer are highlighted in these publi- cations, since the effluent resulting from DA, agronomically, represents an important source of nutrients, not only in terms of food security, due to the depletion of natural sources of phosphorus, for example, but also in environmental terms by replacing the production, use and transport of synthetic fertilizers, responsible for the emission of a significant amount of GHG throughout their life cycle (Walling and Vaneeckhaute, 2020). Consequently, due to the substitution of fossil sources for a renewable matrix, in both scenarios, the environmental gains are perceptible, with mitigations in the global warming potential (GWP), which can exceed -200% (Jiang et al., 2021).

Despite a large amount of information on the subject in the literature, most of these studies do not evaluate existing treatment plants con- cerning electricity generation and the potential for biomethane pro- duction, as a substitute for fossil vehicular fuel (gasoline or diesel), in the Brazilian context of pig farming. Furthermore, the different objec- tives, assumptions, and limits of the systems studied make comparison and extrapolating results difficult. Thus, the present study employs the LCA methodology to evaluate the environmental impacts of the treat- ment of swine manure in terms of AD for electricity generation and biomethane use to understand different waste management systems in
Brazil, their environmental effects and potential improvements offered by each alternative.
13: A growing concern with the depletion of natural resources has encouraged the search for alternative renewable sources for the current energy demand worldwide (Lima et al., 2020). Countries have fostered the implementation of public policies to encourage the use of non-fossil sources to preserve resources and reduce environmental damage (Deng et al., 2020).

The Brazilian government instituted in April 2021 the "Fuel of the Future Program" to propose measures to increase the use of renewable fuels, besides stimulating the national vehicle technology aiming at the

decarbonization of the national transport energy matrix (Brasil, 2021). These measures follow the country's commitment to the Paris agree- ment, where Brazil committed to reducing greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions by 43% until 2030 (Lima et al., 2020). To meet this target, the country projects an increase in sustainable biofuels participation to approximately 18% in the national energy matrix until 2030 (Brasil, 2015, 2021).

The current need to mitigate environmental impacts and simulta- neously be allied with economic development has changed consumption patterns and production chains and linear systems are no longer sus- tainable (Borges et al., 2021). There is a need to change the thought of

waste at the end of the process, incorporating reuse and recycling op- tions in production chains towards a circular and low carbon economy, adding value to waste (Wainaina et al., 2020). In this context, anaerobic digestion (AD) has been one of the technologies currently employed for energy recovery from waste worldwide (Rekleitis et al., 2020). Biogas from AD can be used for energy purposes in several ways, either by replacing liquefied petroleum gas, cogeneration of electricity and heat, or biofuel (Baena-Moreno et al., 2021).
In Brazil, according to data released by the International Center for Renewable Energy (CIBiogas, 2021), agriculture and livestock represent

the primary sources of substrate used for biogas production, corre- sponding to 79% of the AD plants operating in the country as the pri- mary substrate. Livestock has particular relevance. According to estimates by the United States Department of Agriculture and Foreign Agricultural Service (USDA), in 2021, Brazil should be responsible for 23% of global meat exports, configuring itself as an important branch of the national economy (USDA, 2021).

The management of livestock waste is fundamental in searching for a more sustainable future since this type of activity corresponds to about 15% of global anthropogenic GHG emissions. That is, the sector is

responsible for the emission of 7.1 GT CO2 eq. and this value increases every year due to the increase in food demand driven by population growth, which pressures the sector to expand production, highlighting the importance of the adoption of a low carbon economy (FAO, 2017).

Brazil is the fourth-largest producer of pork globally and relies on a confined animal production system as the primary production strategy to meet market demand (USDA, 2021). However, despite the produc- tivity benefits of this configuration, there is the generation of a high volume of effluents concentrated in a small geographical area, which are known for their polluting potential, the GHG emission, eutrophication of watercourses, and impacts on air quality (Tallaksen et al., 2020). To bypass these damages, AD has been one of the allied strategies of pig farming in managing these effluents, allowing energy recovery and reducing GHG emissions. Animal waste can be an essential substrate for the AD in the search for change in the national energy matrix, making this activity environmentally viable towards a country with low carbon meat production (Velásquez Piñas et al., 2019).

While AD is a promising route to waste management, the environ- mental impacts associated with this activity must be carefully analyzed to ensure its environmental viability (Hollas et al., 2021). In this regard, the life cycle assessment (LCA) methodology has been used to examine the environmental impacts of swine waste treatment. Many researches have been dedicated to studying the environmental impacts of different treatment systems aiming at the adequate management of livestock waste combined with the recovery of energy and nutrients resulting from the AD, reporting the impacts and benefits generated by the adopted configurations (Table 1) (Esteves et al., 2019).

According to Table 1, the configuration adopted for the management of livestock waste can have different roles in the production of biogas and consequently in the LCA. this is reflected in the values of the envi- ronmental impacts reported, which present great variability ranging from kg CO2 eq. to tons of CO2 eq. for the biogas generation scenarios. Esteves et al. (2019) conducted a review on LCA studies of manure biogas production system in a global perspective and concluded that there are several aspects that make the comparison of these results critical, according to the authors biogas production presents many variables that make it difficult to compare the results, the technological level of the plants, the sources of energy and raw material, as well as climatic and regional issues, can affect the evaluation, which justifies this variability found in the literature results.

Consequently, due to the substitution of fossil sources for a renewable matrix, in both scenarios, the environmental gains are perceptible, with mitigations in the global warming potential (GWP), which can exceed -200% (Jiang et al., 2021).

Despite a large amount of information on the subject in the literature, most of these studies do not evaluate existing treatment plants con- cerning electricity generation and the potential for biomethane pro- duction, as a substitute for fossil vehicular fuel (gasoline or diesel), in the Brazilian context of pig farming. Furthermore, the different objec- tives, assumptions, and limits of the systems studied make comparison and extrapolating results difficult. Thus, the present study employs the LCA methodology to evaluate the environmental impacts of the treat- ment of swine manure in terms of AD for electricity generation and biomethane use to understand different waste management systems in
Brazil, their environmental effects and potential improvements offered by each alternative.
14: The environmental aspects of five swine production units are eval- uated following the ISO 14040 and 14044 standards (ISO 14040, 2006; ISO 14044, 2006), and the Brazilian ABNT standards (14040 (ABNT, 2014a) and 14044 (ABNT, 2014b)) based on the ISO respectives, with the SimaPro 9.1.1.1 software. LCA presents itself as a decision support technique and can be applied to the waste management model adopted (Mersoni and Reichert, 2017). Considering the entire life cycle of the management system, from generation to final disposal, it can assist governmental organizations in strategic planning and defining treat- ment processes. Therefore, the study uses LCA for quantifying the environmental benefits of the systems under study.
15: The objective of the LCA in this study is to determine and compare the potential environmental impacts of swine waste management for electricity or biomethane production during swine manure treatment by AD for six scenarios. The study covers five swine projects as case studies, three located in the southern region of Brazil (Paraná State) and two in the southeastern region of Brazil (Minas Gerais State). Four of these systems use biogas to produce electricity, and one of them uses biogas to produce biomethane to replace vehicular gasoline (Table 2).

The functional unit adopted is one ton of waste treated. The scope of the study covers manure storage; stabilization of organic matter fol- lowed by field application, and/or production of biogas for electricity or biomethane; followed by storage of the AD digestate and application as fertilizer (Fig. 1).

The baseline includes stabilization of manure in an open lagoon (120 days), transport to the field, and application to the soil as a source of fertilizer (e.g., N and P). Scenario 1 (SM includes AD based on a covered lagoon biodigestor (CLB) and storage of the digestate in an open lagoon (100 days). Then, the digestate is applied as fertilizer to the soil after 100 days of storage, an emission hot spot in LCA studies of biogas systems, and the gaseous fraction (biogas) is converted to electricity (SM 1.1). Complementary to this, for the same scenario, the study simulates that biogas is purified and used to replace diesel fuel with a 12% bio- diesel blend (SM rather than being used for electricity generation.

For Scenario 2 (SM 2), after the AD process (CLB), the effluent is submitted to a solid-liquid separation (SSL) through a sieve. Then, it is divided into two fractions, the liquid one being stored in an open tank (60 days) and then applied to the soil, and the solid one being composted and applied to the soil. The gaseous fraction (biogas) is converted to electricity (SM Similarly to Scenario 1, the study simulates that biomethane replaces diesel fuel with a 12% biodiesel blend (SM 2.2) instead of electricity.

Scenario 3 (SM is similar to Scenario 2. The manure is submitted to AD process (CLB), followed by SSL, composting of the solid fraction and storage of the liquid fraction in an open storage lagoon (60 days), and subsequent land application (both liquid fraction and composted solid fraction). The gaseous fraction (biogas) is used to produce biomethane to replace vehicular gasoline (SM 3.1), and the study simulates that biogas is used to generate electricity (SM 3.2) instead of biomethane.

Scenario 4 (SM comprises a treatment plant that receives effluents from 40 swine farms in the region. The waste is transported to the unit by trucks and submitted to AD based on a CLB, followed by storage of the digestate in an open lagoon (60 days) and land application. The gaseous fraction (biogas) is converted to electricity (SM 4.1), and like other scenarios, the study simulates that biogas is purified into biomethane and used to replace diesel fuel 12% biodiesel blend (SM 4.2) instead of electricity.
Scenario 5 (SM5) comprises a power generation unit SM5.1), which

scenario|Number of animals (production system)|Installed capacity|Swine manure|Biogas gproduction|Electric energy generation|Biomethane production SM 1|4E+03 (Finishing)||360 m³ d-Superscript(1)|480 m³ d|30 MWh month| SM 2|12E+03 in termination/7E+03 nursery|250 kWh|300 m³ d-|2.1E+03 m³ d 1|4.5E+03 kWh d-Superscript(1)| SM 3|1.4E+03 SOWS|36 m³ h-1a|110 m³ d-Superscript(1)|||220 m³ d 1 SM 4|Manure of 40 swine farms|-|448 m³ d-Superscript(1)|10E+03 m³|600 MWh month| SM 5|Manure of 17 small properties|250 MWh|243 m³ 1|5.23E+03 m³ d|10.21E+03 kWh d 1|

receives the biogas generated from 17 small farms and stores it in gas- ometers. These small properties have covered lagoon biodigester and lagoon for storing the digestate (60 days of storage) and digestate land application. A pipeline is used to transport the biogas from the 17 properties, approximately 22 km, to an energy recovery plant. The study simulates that biogas is purified and used to replace diesel fuel with a 12% biodiesel blend (SM 5.2) rather than electricity generation. For all scenarios, impacts related to the construction stages of the physical facilities are not included in the evaluation, as they do not arise from changes in manure management. Thus, they are out of the study's scope.

19: The objective of the LCA in this study is to determine and compare the potential environmental impacts of swine waste management for electricity or biomethane production during swine manure treatment by AD for six scenarios. The study covers five swine projects as case studies, three located in the southern region of Brazil (Paraná State) and two in the southeastern region of Brazil (Minas Gerais State). Four of these systems use biogas to produce electricity, and one of them uses biogas to produce biomethane to replace vehicular gasoline (Table 2).

The functional unit adopted is one ton of waste treated. The scope of the study covers manure storage; stabilization of organic matter fol- lowed by field application, and/or production of biogas for electricity or biomethane; followed by storage of the AD digestate and application as fertilizer (Fig. 1).

The baseline includes stabilization of manure in an open lagoon (120 days), transport to the field, and application to the soil as a source of fertilizer (e.g., N and P). Scenario 1 (SM includes AD based on a covered lagoon biodigestor (CLB) and storage of the digestate in an open lagoon (100 days). Then, the digestate is applied as fertilizer to the soil after 100 days of storage, an emission hot spot in LCA studies of biogas systems, and the gaseous fraction (biogas) is converted to electricity (SM 1.1). Complementary to this, for the same scenario, the study simulates that biogas is purified and used to replace diesel fuel with a 12% bio- diesel blend (SM rather than being used for electricity generation.

For Scenario 2 (SM 2), after the AD process (CLB), the effluent is submitted to a solid-liquid separation (SSL) through a sieve. Then, it is divided into two fractions, the liquid one being stored in an open tank (60 days) and then applied to the soil, and the solid one being composted and applied to the soil. The gaseous fraction (biogas) is converted to electricity (SM Similarly to Scenario 1, the study simulates that biomethane replaces diesel fuel with a 12% biodiesel blend (SM 2.2) instead of electricity.

Scenario 3 (SM is similar to Scenario 2. The manure is submitted to AD process (CLB), followed by SSL, composting of the solid fraction and storage of the liquid fraction in an open storage lagoon (60 days), and subsequent land application (both liquid fraction and composted solid fraction). The gaseous fraction (biogas) is used to produce biomethane to replace vehicular gasoline (SM 3.1), and the study simulates that biogas is used to generate electricity (SM 3.2) instead of biomethane.

Scenario 4 (SM comprises a treatment plant that receives effluents from 40 swine farms in the region. The waste is transported to the unit by trucks and submitted to AD based on a CLB, followed by storage of the digestate in an open lagoon (60 days) and land application. The gaseous fraction (biogas) is converted to electricity (SM 4.1), and like other scenarios, the study simulates that biogas is purified into biomethane and used to replace diesel fuel 12% biodiesel blend (SM 4.2) instead of electricity.

21: <header>2.2. Life-cycle inventory </header>

The construction of the life cycle inventory (LCI) allows us to un- derstand the magnitude and significance of potential environmental impacts of waste residues. LCI considers each phase's processes and their corresponding inputs and outputs, emissions, and energy demand. The analysis uses data from Ecoinvent 3.7.1 database (Ecoinvent, 2020) relevant literature, and information collected directly in the systems under study.

The baseline inventory (Table 3), concerning storage, estimates CH4 emissions based on the model presented by Sardá et al. (2018) and NH3 emissions based on the model presented by Kunz and Mukhtar (2016),
Table 3 Life cycle inventories for Baseline scenarios (mass balances per 1 ton of manure).

Inputs|Baseline Manure, in a ton|1.0 Outputs|Outputs Product avoided|Product avoided Chemical fertilizer N, kg|2.93 Chemical fertilizer P2O5, in kg|1.83 Emissions to air|Emissions to air CH4 in storage, in kg|3.73 NH3 in storage, in kg|0.17 NH3 in spreading, in kg|0.82 N2O in spreading, in kg|0.04 NO in spreading, in kg|0.32 Emissions to water|Emissions to water NO3 leaching, in kg|1.16 P leaching, in kg|4.20E-03 Emissions to soil|Emissions to soil Copper, in g|8.98 Zinc, in g|63.58 Aluminium, in mg|1.13E+05 Cadmium, in mg|8.90 Chromium, in mg|346.00 Lead, in mg|6.70

the models are described in the supplementary material. According to the emission factors presented by Cherubini et al. (2015), emissions from the agricultural application are estimated using nitrogen, ammonia, zinc, copper, aluminium, cadmium, chromium, lead and phosphorus concentration typical of swine manure, which is also described in the supplementary material.

Soil application of the manure in the baseline scenario considers fertilization by tractor, with an application rate of 80 m³ ha (Ecoin- vent, 2020). For all scenarios, the Ecoinvent database presents the avoided impacts from electricity and/or fuel production and fertilizers replacement, adjusted based on the nitrogen and phosphorus content of the manure/digestate.

2.2.1. Life cycle inventory for the perspective of electricity generation The inventory of electricity generation from biogas (Table 4) relies on the Ecoinvent database (Ecoinvent, 2020). Electricity data consider regional differences in the Brazilian electricity generation mix and its associated impacts. Thus, the analysis considers values from the South and Southeast regions where the case studies are located. Ecoinvent database (Ecoinvent, 2020) also provides data from effluent transport to the biodigester - when necessary - and energy demands and emissions related to the motor-generator for electric energy generation and ferti- gation. The avoided impacts of electricity and phosphate and nitrogen fertilizers are considered, as described above.

The emissions from the digestate storage lagoon (i.e., CH4 and NH3 emissions) and characteristics of the manure and respective digestates are estimated according to the models presented in the supplementary material. Fugitive emissions from the AD process were determined based on the values presented by Scheutz and Fredenslund (2019), who noted that on average agricultural AD plants have a loss of 2.4% of the biogas generated. In the stage of manure soil application, sprinkler fertilization by an electric pump is considered with an application rate of 80 m³ ha-1.
2.2.2. Life cycle inventory for the biomethane generation perspective

The inventory of the biomethane generation (Table 5) follows the same approach adopted by the inventory of electricity generation from biogas previously described. Data from biogas purification (pressure swing adsorption process (PSA) was considered), compression, and decompression processes consider Jungbluth et al. (2007) and Ecoinvent database (Ecoinvent, 2020). The biogas purification process considers the methane content in biogas, and the compression and decompression consider energy loss. The emissions from the digestate storage lagoon (i.

2.2.1. Life cycle inventory for the perspective of electricity generation The inventory of electricity generation from biogas (Table 4) relies on the Ecoinvent database (Ecoinvent, 2020). The emissions from the digestate storage lagoon (i.e., CH4 and NH3 emissions) and characteristics of the manure and respective digestates are estimated according to the models presented in the supplementary material. Fugitive emissions from the AD process were determined based on the values presented by Scheutz and Fredenslund (2019), who noted that on average agricultural AD plants have a loss of 2.4% of the biogas generated. In the stage of manure soil application, sprinkler fertilization by an electric pump is considered with an application rate of 80 m³ ha-1.

The inventory of the biomethane generation (Table 5) follows the same approach adopted by the inventory of electricity generation from biogas previously described. Data from biogas purification (pressure swing adsorption process (PSA) was considered), compression, and decompression processes consider Jungbluth et al. (2007) and Ecoinvent database (Ecoinvent, 2020). The biogas purification process considers the methane content in biogas, and the compression and decompression consider energy loss. 27: <header>2.2. Life-cycle inventory </header>

The construction of the life cycle inventory (LCI) allows us to un- derstand the magnitude and significance of potential environmental impacts of waste residues. LCI considers each phase's processes and their corresponding inputs and outputs, emissions, and energy demand. The analysis uses data from Ecoinvent 3.7.1 database (Ecoinvent, 2020) relevant literature, and information collected directly in the systems under study.

The baseline inventory (Table 3), concerning storage, estimates CH4 emissions based on the model presented by Sardá et al. (2018) and NH3 emissions based on the model presented by Kunz and Mukhtar (2016),

Soil application of the manure in the baseline scenario considers fertilization by tractor, with an application rate of 80 m³ ha (Ecoin- vent, 2020). Ecoinvent database (Ecoinvent, 2020) also provides data from effluent transport to the biodigester - when necessary - and energy demands and emissions related to the motor-generator for electric energy generation and ferti- gation. In the stage of manure soil application, sprinkler fertilization by an electric pump is considered with an application rate of 80 m³ ha-1.

The 1st National Inventory of Atmospheric Emissions by Road Motor Vehicles (MMA, 2011) presents the emission factors (see Table A6 in supplementary material) to avoided diesel emissions. Emissions from biomethane in vehicles are used from the Ecoinvent database as pre- sented by Jungbluth et al. (2007). The avoided impact of diesel pro- duction is also considered from the Ecoinvent database (Ecoinvent, 2020), which includes adapting it to the Brazilian context in which diesel fuel has a 12% biodiesel blend. For case studies using biomethane to replace gasoline, the Ecoinvent inventory is adapted considering the 27% ethanol blend in the Brazilian gasoline. For soil application of the waste, it follows the same approach adopted by the inventory of elec- tricity generation from biogas.
30: <header>2.2. Life-cycle inventory </header>

The construction of the life cycle inventory (LCI) allows us to un- derstand the magnitude and significance of potential environmental impacts of waste residues. LCI considers each phase's processes and their corresponding inputs and outputs, emissions, and energy demand. The analysis uses data from Ecoinvent 3.7.1 database (Ecoinvent, 2020) relevant literature, and information collected directly in the systems under study.

The baseline inventory (Table 3), concerning storage, estimates CH4 emissions based on the model presented by Sardá et al. (2018) and NH3 emissions based on the model presented by Kunz and Mukhtar (2016),

the models are described in the supplementary material. According to the emission factors presented by Cherubini et al. (2015), emissions from the agricultural application are estimated using nitrogen, ammonia, zinc, copper, aluminium, cadmium, chromium, lead and phosphorus concentration typical of swine manure, which is also described in the supplementary material.

Soil application of the manure in the baseline scenario considers fertilization by tractor, with an application rate of 80 m³ ha (Ecoin- vent, 2020). For all scenarios, the Ecoinvent database presents the avoided impacts from electricity and/or fuel production and fertilizers replacement, adjusted based on the nitrogen and phosphorus content of the manure/digestate.

2.2.1. Life cycle inventory for the perspective of electricity generation The inventory of electricity generation from biogas (Table 4) relies on the Ecoinvent database (Ecoinvent, 2020). Electricity data consider regional differences in the Brazilian electricity generation mix and its associated impacts. Thus, the analysis considers values from the South and Southeast regions where the case studies are located. Ecoinvent database (Ecoinvent, 2020) also provides data from effluent transport to the biodigester - when necessary - and energy demands and emissions related to the motor-generator for electric energy generation and ferti- gation. The avoided impacts of electricity and phosphate and nitrogen fertilizers are considered, as described above.

The emissions from the digestate storage lagoon (i.e., CH4 and NH3 emissions) and characteristics of the manure and respective digestates are estimated according to the models presented in the supplementary material. Fugitive emissions from the AD process were determined based on the values presented by Scheutz and Fredenslund (2019), who noted that on average agricultural AD plants have a loss of 2.4% of the biogas generated. In the stage of manure soil application, sprinkler fertilization by an electric pump is considered with an application rate of 80 m³ ha-1.
2.2.2. Life cycle inventory for the biomethane generation perspective

The inventory of the biomethane generation (Table 5) follows the same approach adopted by the inventory of electricity generation from biogas previously described. Data from biogas purification (pressure swing adsorption process (PSA) was considered), compression, and decompression processes consider Jungbluth et al. (2007) and Ecoinvent database (Ecoinvent, 2020). The biogas purification process considers the methane content in biogas, and the compression and decompression consider energy loss. The emissions from the digestate storage lagoon (i.

36: The methodology for assessing environmental impacts uses ReCiPe Midpoint 2016. The ReCiPe is a problem-oriented, globally scoped method for impact assessment (Huijbregts et al., 2017). Considering the most urgent environmental issues in the context of Brazilian livestock farming, the following impact categories were evaluated: (i) global warming; (ii) stratospheric ozone depletion; (iii) ozone formation - terrestrial ecosystem; (iv) terrestrial acidification; (v) freshwater eutrophication, and (vi) terrestrial ecotoxicity.
The result interpretation follows the recommendations presented by
Zampori et al. (2016) in the "Guide for interpreting the result of life cycle assessment", which defines the significant aspects to be considered when interpreting the results.
37: The use of databases and literature data can lead to uncertainties in LCA studies, as can the estimates made to determine the actual emission values for each scenario, due to this an uncertainty analysis of the data used was performed. To quantify the uncertainty related to data vari- ability and quality, a pedigree matrix was applied using the Ecoinvent data quality system in SimaPro (Ciroth et al., 2016). A Monte Carlo simulation (1000 runs) of the foreground system was performed, to quantify the uncertainties for the results with 95% confinement interval.
38: 3.1. The environmental impact of the swine manure management scenarios from the perspective of electricity generation

The environmental performance of the scenarios is variable in Table 6, which shows the Baseline Scenario having the worst environ- mental performance in almost all categories, except for freshwater eutrophication and for terrestrial ecotoxicity, where it has the lowest impact.

According to Duan et al. (2020), the swine waste management model is one of the most advantageous treatment systems since its effluent treatment uses AD, which produces by-products (e.g., biogas and fertil- izer) that can reduce operational costs in the long run. The authors also performed an LCA of different scenarios to treat animal waste using AD and obtained environmentally favourable results.

Analyzing the baseline results it is possible to verify that for the global warming category, the stage that contributes more significantly is the storage of the manure for 120 days, emitting 3.73 kg of CH4 per tonne of waste and representing 80% of the total value of this category (e.g., 119.25 kg CO2 eq) as illustrated in Fig. 2. The fertilization stage, which comprises the application of the digestate to the soil, contributes the most in the other impact categories, being the only one that affects the stratospheric ozone depletion category (e.g., 2.97E-04 kg CFC11 eq). Nutrient recovery can mitigate up to 99% of the impacts of terrestrial ecotoxicity, 25% of the stratospheric ozone depletion, and 10% of global warming due to the replacement of nitrogen fertilizers.

For the other scenarios, the storage stage in the digestate lagoons is also one of the stages that most affects the global warming category. In SM 1.1, the lagoon emissions contribute 91.67% of a total of 32.16 kg CO2 eq and the time of 100 days is a factor that negatively influences this system, causing a higher emission than the other scenarios. Estimates released by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency in the inventory of GHG Emissions and Sinks (EPA's, 2021) show that for the year 2019, the emissions of manure management in the country were 81.98 million tonnes CO2 eq (MMT CO2 eq), being the stages of treatment, storage, and transportation of livestock manure those that have most contributed to this value. Thus, the mitigation of GHG emissions associated with ani- mal waste storage are relevant factors in developing a treatment scheme, and reducing the storage time can reduce GHG emissions (Im et al., 2020).

For SM 2.1, the storage stage represents 36.19% of the total impacts of the global warming category, while in SM 3.1, the lagoons emit 12.44 kg CO2 eq or 38.56% of the total. For SM 5.1, the fertilization stage and the storage of digestate in the lagoons contribute with 15.78 1 0.62% of the global warming impact, in addition, fugitive emissions from the AD plant contribute 16.5% of the impact. SM 4.1 is the exception, where the most significant contribution to the global warming category is due to three reasons: (i) the transport of manure to the plant (e.g., 5.02 kgCO2eq); (ii) the CO2 emission from burning of fossil fuel (e.g., 4.93 kgCO2eq); (iii) and fugitive emissions from the AD plant (e.g., 8.29

The stage of digestate land application (i.e., fertigation) is the one that causes the most adverse impacts in almost all scenarios. The ferti- gation process is based on the application of liquid digestate through sprinkling, having a considerable loss of nutrients through volatilization and leaching, which justifies the significant influence of this step on the categories under study. According to Finzi et al. (2019), the fertigation process significantly impacts pollutant emissions; the authors found that processes based on the injection of digestate into the soil instead of sprinkling reduce NH3 emissions to 60% compared to the broadcast spreading process. Hence, the choice of application technology is one of the critical factors for mitigating these impacts.

In the Baseline Scenario, fertigation is responsible for 75.32% of the impacts in stratospheric ozone depletion, 94.87% in ozone formation - terrestrial ecosystems, 81.23% in terrestrial acidification, and 56.48% in freshwater eutrophication. Besides the spreading process, the burning of diesel due to transport for the application of fertilizers also negatively affects this process, making the other scenarios presenting a lower impact in this stage because it uses a tractor for fertilizing instead of an electric pump. The use of fossil fuels, due to the transport of waste to the
treatment plant, is also responsible for the most significant impact in the terrestrial ecotoxicity category of SM 4.1, which corresponds to 85.11% of the total impacts.

SM 2.1 and SM 3.1 show an increase in the impact categories caused by the fertigation process due to liquid and solid digestate application, which implies two processes contributing to impacts. However, all sce- narios present similar results about the adverse impacts in the different categories analyzed, given the differences in waste management in each treatment unit. The minor differences observed can be justified by the characteristics of each waste, such as nutrient concentration and organic matter, which in turn affects emissions (Orner et al., 2021).

Despite high impacts in five scenarios, benefits from the recovery of fertilizers (i.e., N and P) and energy can mitigate most of them. In SM 2.1, the avoided impacts in the global warming category represent 39.96% of the total. They are -3.24 kg CO2 eq. of avoided electricity consumption, -8.72 kg CO2 eq. of avoided N fertilizer consumption, and -1.77 kg CO2 eq. of avoided superfostate fertilizer consumption.

In SM 3.1, the values are similar, with the avoided impacts repre- senting 39.54% of the total, of which -9.15 kg CO2 eq from avoided N consumption; -1.83 kg CO2 eq. from avoided electricity demand, and -1.77 kg CO2 eq. from avoided superfostate fertilizer consumption. In SM 4.1, the contribution of avoided N fertilizer is 27.41% of the total and 16.31% of the total from avoided electricity consumption.

In SM 5.1, the avoided production of N fertilizer has a significant contribution as a negative impact, contributing -15.55 kg CO2 eq or 35.93% of the total. Besides global warming, terrestrial ecotoxicity and stratospheric ozone depletion also improve their environmental per- formance due to nutrient and energy recovery processes.

The greatest uncertainty of the data was in relation to the Freshwater eutrophication (kg P eq) category, where the average value was outside the confidence interval, for almost all scenarios, with the exception of SM 1.1 (Fig. 2) (Table A7 in supplementary material). Niero et al. (2015) also verified this behavior by analyzing the uncertainty of their data, according to the authors the estimate of P leaching to the environment is the main cause of variation in the scenarios due to the uncertainty that this estimate causes. In addition to uncertainties realigned to estimates that compromise data integrity, background inventories, such as from Ecoinvent and literature data, are the main sources of uncertainty in the LCI values (Esteves et al., 2019), but the other categories showed average values within the established (95%) confidence interval.

The results show that the replacement of fertilizers is the process that has the most relevant contribution to reducing associated environmental impacts. Unlike Ramirez-Islas et al. (2020), claiming that the avoided electricity consumption presented the highest contributions (e.g., 82-93%) for the mitigation of climate change category in the LCA of swine manure management, this is due to the energy matrix considered by the authors relies on fossil fuels, raising the environmental benefits of waste energy recovery. As the Brazilian energy matrix is based on hy- droelectric, the benefits of energy recovery have less significant values than the recovery of nutrients (Deng et al., 2020).
3.2. The environmental impact of the scenarios of swine manure management in the perspective of biomethane generation

The environmental performance results of scenarios that have bio- methane as a product instead of electricity are presented in Table 7. They follow the same trend observed in scenarios for electricity gener- ation presented previously. The baseline is the scenario that presented the highest environmental impacts, except for freshwater eutrophication and terrestrial ecotoxicity, which has the best environmental performance.

Emissions from storage ponds are the most significant contributor to the global warming category due to the emission of CH4 in almost all scenarios (see Fig. 3). In SM 1.2, storage contributes 84.17% of the emissions. SM 2.2 accounts for 24.36% of the total or 12.44 kg CO2 eq. Finally, SM 3.2 is responsible for 38.56% of the total.

In SM 4.2 and SM 5.2, biogas purification steps, and fugitive emis- sions from the AD plant, contribute significantly to the global warming category; this steps accounts for 28.65 kg CO2 or 29.62% of the total category in SM 4.2, while in SM 5.2, biogas purification is responsible for the emission of 17.44 kg CO2 eq and fugitive emissions from the AD plant are responsible for the emission of 7.02 kg CO2 eq. In SM 2.2, biogas purification also has a considerable contribution, corresponding to 6.14 kg CO2 or 12.03% of the total, and in SM 3.2, it is equivalent to 2.59 kg CO2 eq. Rasi et al. (2020) found that the gas purification stage

was responsible for the emission of 10.6 gCO2eq MJ according to the authors, the energy consumption of the process was the leading cause of high emissions; whereas Van den Oever et al. (2021) discuss that the emissions of this stage are negligible compared to those of the storage stage and its contribution to the climate change category. In addition, the digestate management should consider reducing the storage time or effect, such as considering the use of closed tanks or adopting treatment techniques such as solid-liquid separation or acidification, which contribute to the reduction of GHG emissions (Jiang et al., 2021).

The soil application stage is the most significant contributor to the negative impacts of stratospheric ozone depletion, ozone formation - terrestrial ecosystem, freshwater eutrophication, and terrestrial acidifi- cation categories, except in SM 4.2, where the terrestrial ecotoxicity category is the most significant contributor. This result in SM 4.2 is from the transportation of manure to the biodigestion plant, caused by the emission of copper by the brake wear process of trucks (e.g., 64.05 kg 1.4-BCD) (Hulskotte et al., 2007). Among these categories, N2O emission is the leading cause of negative environmental performance in the stratospheric ozone depletion category. In contrast, ammonia emission is responsible for terrestrial acidification. Freshwater eutrophication is affected by phosphorus emission, and NO emission impacts ozone for- mation - terrestrial ecosystem. These pollutants are inherent in fertil- ization processes, whether as a function of digestate emissions or application mechanisms (Orner et al., 2021).

Similar to the electricity generation scenarios, the avoided impacts due to the substitution products (e.g., biomethane replacing diesel or gasoline) significantly contribute to the final impact values. In SM 2.2, the avoided impacts represent 47.74% of the total global warming category, being -12.19 kg CO2 eq. from avoided diesel-burning, -1.70 kg CO2 eq. from avoided diesel production, -8.72 kg CO2 eq. from avoided N fertilizer, and -1.77 kg CO2 eq. from avoided superfosfast fertilizer. In SM 3.2, the terrestrial ecotoxicity category presents a negative impact value (e.g., -13.51 kg 1.4-DCB) due to the avoided chemical fertilizer use, representing 73.55% (e.g., - 10.52 kg 1.4-DCB), and the avoided gasoline representing 23.68% or -3.39 kg 1.4-DCB. In SM 5.2, the avoided emissions from diesel-burning represent the highest percentage of negative impacts, equivalent to 36.74% or -34.65 kg CO2 eq, for the global warming category.

Similar to the power generation scenarios, the Freshwater eutro- phication category (kg P eq) is the one that presents the greatest un- certainties in relation to the LCI data, with values outside the confidence interval (95%), and the greatest error associated with the data (Fig. 3) (Table A8 in supplementary material). The Terrestrial ecotoxicity cate- gory is the second with the greatest uncertainty in the data, which is directly related to the variability of the metals in the swine waste, ac- cording to Steinmetz et al. (2009) in the period of one year of monitoring the metals in the swine waste the variations reached 47% between the minimum and maximum value, which directly reflects the uncertainties presented here.

According to Ou et al. (2012), the use of natural gas, which is similar to purified biomethane, leads to 5-10% lower GHG emissions than gasoline and diesel-powered vehicles, respectively. Zhang et al. (2020)

39: 3.1. The environmental impact of the swine manure management scenarios from the perspective of electricity generation

The environmental performance of the scenarios is variable in Table 6, which shows the Baseline Scenario having the worst environ- mental performance in almost all categories, except for freshwater eutrophication and for terrestrial ecotoxicity, where it has the lowest impact.

According to Duan et al. (2020), the swine waste management model is one of the most advantageous treatment systems since its effluent treatment uses AD, which produces by-products (e.g., biogas and fertil- izer) that can reduce operational costs in the long run. The fertilization stage, which comprises the application of the digestate to the soil, contributes the most in the other impact categories, being the only one that affects the stratospheric ozone depletion category (e.g., 2.97E-04 kg CFC11 eq). Nutrient recovery can mitigate up to 99% of the impacts of terrestrial ecotoxicity, 25% of the stratospheric ozone depletion, and 10% of global warming due to the replacement of nitrogen fertilizers.

For the other scenarios, the storage stage in the digestate lagoons is also one of the stages that most affects the global warming category. In SM 1.1, the lagoon emissions contribute 91.67% of a total of 32.16 kg CO2 eq and the time of 100 days is a factor that negatively influences this system, causing a higher emission than the other scenarios. Estimates released by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency in the inventory of GHG Emissions and Sinks (EPA's, 2021) show that for the year 2019, the emissions of manure management in the country were 81.98 million tonnes CO2 eq (MMT CO2 eq), being the stages of treatment, storage, and transportation of livestock manure those that have most contributed to this value. Thus, the mitigation of GHG emissions associated with ani- mal waste storage are relevant factors in developing a treatment scheme, and reducing the storage time can reduce GHG emissions (Im et al., 2020).

For SM 2.1, the storage stage represents 36.19% of the total impacts of the global warming category, while in SM 3.1, the lagoons emit 12.44 kg CO2 eq or 38.56% of the total. For SM 5.1, the fertilization stage and the storage of digestate in the lagoons contribute with 15.78 1 0.62% of the global warming impact, in addition, fugitive emissions from the AD plant contribute 16.5% of the impact. SM 4.1 is the exception, where the most significant contribution to the global warming category is due to three reasons: (i) the transport of manure to the plant (e.g., 5.02 kgCO2eq); (ii) the CO2 emission from burning of fossil fuel (e.g., 4.93 kgCO2eq); (iii) and fugitive emissions from the AD plant (e.g., 8.29
