kgCO2eq). The fugitive emissions are important in the context of envi- ronmental assessment, and can correspond to a significant percentage of biogas loss, contributing directly to the environmental impact, as observed in this study (Scheutz and Fredenslund, 2019).

The stage of digestate land application (i.e., fertigation) is the one that causes the most adverse impacts in almost all scenarios. The ferti- gation process is based on the application of liquid digestate through sprinkling, having a considerable loss of nutrients through volatilization and leaching, which justifies the significant influence of this step on the categories under study. According to Finzi et al. (2019), the fertigation process significantly impacts pollutant emissions; the authors found that processes based on the injection of digestate into the soil instead of sprinkling reduce NH3 emissions to 60% compared to the broadcast spreading process. Hence, the choice of application technology is one of the critical factors for mitigating these impacts.

In the Baseline Scenario, fertigation is responsible for 75.32% of the impacts in stratospheric ozone depletion, 94.87% in ozone formation - terrestrial ecosystems, 81.23% in terrestrial acidification, and 56.48% in freshwater eutrophication. Besides the spreading process, the burning of diesel due to transport for the application of fertilizers also negatively affects this process, making the other scenarios presenting a lower impact in this stage because it uses a tractor for fertilizing instead of an electric pump. The use of fossil fuels, due to the transport of waste to the
treatment plant, is also responsible for the most significant impact in the terrestrial ecotoxicity category of SM 4.1, which corresponds to 85.11% of the total impacts.

SM 2.1 and SM 3.1 show an increase in the impact categories caused by the fertigation process due to liquid and solid digestate application, which implies two processes contributing to impacts. However, all sce- narios present similar results about the adverse impacts in the different categories analyzed, given the differences in waste management in each treatment unit. The minor differences observed can be justified by the characteristics of each waste, such as nutrient concentration and organic matter, which in turn affects emissions (Orner et al., 2021).

Despite high impacts in five scenarios, benefits from the recovery of fertilizers (i.e., N and P) and energy can mitigate most of them. In SM 2.1, the avoided impacts in the global warming category represent 39.96% of the total. They are -3.24 kg CO2 eq. of avoided electricity consumption, -8.72 kg CO2 eq. of avoided N fertilizer consumption, and -1.77 kg CO2 eq. of avoided superfostate fertilizer consumption.

In SM 3.1, the values are similar, with the avoided impacts repre- senting 39.54% of the total, of which -9.15 kg CO2 eq from avoided N consumption; -1.83 kg CO2 eq. from avoided electricity demand, and -1.77 kg CO2 eq. from avoided superfostate fertilizer consumption. In SM 4.1, the contribution of avoided N fertilizer is 27.41% of the total and 16.31% of the total from avoided electricity consumption.

In SM 5.1, the avoided production of N fertilizer has a significant contribution as a negative impact, contributing -15.55 kg CO2 eq or 35.93% of the total. Besides global warming, terrestrial ecotoxicity and stratospheric ozone depletion also improve their environmental per- formance due to nutrient and energy recovery processes.

The greatest uncertainty of the data was in relation to the Freshwater eutrophication (kg P eq) category, where the average value was outside the confidence interval, for almost all scenarios, with the exception of SM 1.1 (Fig. 2) (Table A7 in supplementary material). Niero et al. (2015) also verified this behavior by analyzing the uncertainty of their data, according to the authors the estimate of P leaching to the environment is the main cause of variation in the scenarios due to the uncertainty that this estimate causes. In addition to uncertainties realigned to estimates that compromise data integrity, background inventories, such as from Ecoinvent and literature data, are the main sources of uncertainty in the LCI values (Esteves et al., 2019), but the other categories showed average values within the established (95%) confidence interval.

The results show that the replacement of fertilizers is the process that has the most relevant contribution to reducing associated environmental impacts. Unlike Ramirez-Islas et al. (2020), claiming that the avoided electricity consumption presented the highest contributions (e.g., 82-93%) for the mitigation of climate change category in the LCA of swine manure management, this is due to the energy matrix considered by the authors relies on fossil fuels, raising the environmental benefits of waste energy recovery. As the Brazilian energy matrix is based on hy- droelectric, the benefits of energy recovery have less significant values than the recovery of nutrients (Deng et al., 2020).
3.2. The environmental impact of the scenarios of swine manure management in the perspective of biomethane generation

The environmental performance results of scenarios that have bio- methane as a product instead of electricity are presented in Table 7. They follow the same trend observed in scenarios for electricity gener- ation presented previously. The baseline is the scenario that presented the highest environmental impacts, except for freshwater eutrophication and terrestrial ecotoxicity, which has the best environmental performance.

Emissions from storage ponds are the most significant contributor to the global warming category due to the emission of CH4 in almost all scenarios (see Fig. 3). In SM 1.2, storage contributes 84.17% of the emissions. SM 2.2 accounts for 24.36% of the total or 12.44 kg CO2 eq. Finally, SM 3.2 is responsible for 38.56% of the total.

In SM 4.2 and SM 5.2, biogas purification steps, and fugitive emis- sions from the AD plant, contribute significantly to the global warming category; this steps accounts for 28.65 kg CO2 or 29.62% of the total category in SM 4.2, while in SM 5.2, biogas purification is responsible for the emission of 17.44 kg CO2 eq and fugitive emissions from the AD plant are responsible for the emission of 7.02 kg CO2 eq. In SM 2.2, biogas purification also has a considerable contribution, corresponding to 6.14 kg CO2 or 12.03% of the total, and in SM 3.2, it is equivalent to 2.59 kg CO2 eq. Rasi et al. (2020) found that the gas purification stage

was responsible for the emission of 10.6 gCO2eq MJ according to the authors, the energy consumption of the process was the leading cause of high emissions; whereas Van den Oever et al. (2021) discuss that the emissions of this stage are negligible compared to those of the storage stage and its contribution to the climate change category. In addition, the digestate management should consider reducing the storage time or effect, such as considering the use of closed tanks or adopting treatment techniques such as solid-liquid separation or acidification, which contribute to the reduction of GHG emissions (Jiang et al., 2021).

The soil application stage is the most significant contributor to the negative impacts of stratospheric ozone depletion, ozone formation - terrestrial ecosystem, freshwater eutrophication, and terrestrial acidifi- cation categories, except in SM 4.2, where the terrestrial ecotoxicity category is the most significant contributor. This result in SM 4.2 is from the transportation of manure to the biodigestion plant, caused by the emission of copper by the brake wear process of trucks (e.g., 64.05 kg 1.4-BCD) (Hulskotte et al., 2007). Among these categories, N2O emission is the leading cause of negative environmental performance in the stratospheric ozone depletion category. In contrast, ammonia emission is responsible for terrestrial acidification. Freshwater eutrophication is affected by phosphorus emission, and NO emission impacts ozone for- mation - terrestrial ecosystem. These pollutants are inherent in fertil- ization processes, whether as a function of digestate emissions or application mechanisms (Orner et al., 2021).

Similar to the electricity generation scenarios, the avoided impacts due to the substitution products (e.g., biomethane replacing diesel or gasoline) significantly contribute to the final impact values. In SM 2.2, the avoided impacts represent 47.74% of the total global warming category, being -12.19 kg CO2 eq. from avoided diesel-burning, -1.70 kg CO2 eq. from avoided diesel production, -8.72 kg CO2 eq. from avoided N fertilizer, and -1.77 kg CO2 eq. from avoided superfosfast fertilizer. In SM 3.2, the terrestrial ecotoxicity category presents a negative impact value (e.g., -13.51 kg 1.4-DCB) due to the avoided chemical fertilizer use, representing 73.55% (e.g., - 10.52 kg 1.4-DCB), and the avoided gasoline representing 23.68% or -3.39 kg 1.4-DCB. In SM 5.2, the avoided emissions from diesel-burning represent the highest percentage of negative impacts, equivalent to 36.74% or -34.65 kg CO2 eq, for the global warming category.

Similar to the power generation scenarios, the Freshwater eutro- phication category (kg P eq) is the one that presents the greatest un- certainties in relation to the LCI data, with values outside the confidence interval (95%), and the greatest error associated with the data (Fig. 3) (Table A8 in supplementary material). The Terrestrial ecotoxicity cate- gory is the second with the greatest uncertainty in the data, which is directly related to the variability of the metals in the swine waste, ac- cording to Steinmetz et al. (2009) in the period of one year of monitoring the metals in the swine waste the variations reached 47% between the minimum and maximum value, which directly reflects the uncertainties presented here.

According to Ou et al. (2012), the use of natural gas, which is similar to purified biomethane, leads to 5-10% lower GHG emissions than gasoline and diesel-powered vehicles, respectively. Zhang et al. (2020)

60: 3.1. The environmental impact of the swine manure management scenarios from the perspective of electricity generation

The environmental performance of the scenarios is variable in Table 6, which shows the Baseline Scenario having the worst environ- mental performance in almost all categories, except for freshwater eutrophication and for terrestrial ecotoxicity, where it has the lowest impact.

According to Duan et al. (2020), the swine waste management model is one of the most advantageous treatment systems since its effluent treatment uses AD, which produces by-products (e.g., biogas and fertil- izer) that can reduce operational costs in the long run. The authors also performed an LCA of different scenarios to treat animal waste using AD and obtained environmentally favourable results.

Analyzing the baseline results it is possible to verify that for the global warming category, the stage that contributes more significantly is the storage of the manure for 120 days, emitting 3.73 kg of CH4 per tonne of waste and representing 80% of the total value of this category (e.g., 119.25 kg CO2 eq) as illustrated in Fig. 2. The fertilization stage, which comprises the application of the digestate to the soil, contributes the most in the other impact categories, being the only one that affects the stratospheric ozone depletion category (e.g., 2.97E-04 kg CFC11 eq). Nutrient recovery can mitigate up to 99% of the impacts of terrestrial ecotoxicity, 25% of the stratospheric ozone depletion, and 10% of global warming due to the replacement of nitrogen fertilizers.

For the other scenarios, the storage stage in the digestate lagoons is also one of the stages that most affects the global warming category. In SM 1.1, the lagoon emissions contribute 91.67% of a total of 32.16 kg CO2 eq and the time of 100 days is a factor that negatively influences this system, causing a higher emission than the other scenarios. Estimates released by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency in the inventory of GHG Emissions and Sinks (EPA's, 2021) show that for the year 2019, the emissions of manure management in the country were 81.98 million tonnes CO2 eq (MMT CO2 eq), being the stages of treatment, storage, and transportation of livestock manure those that have most contributed to this value. Thus, the mitigation of GHG emissions associated with ani- mal waste storage are relevant factors in developing a treatment scheme, and reducing the storage time can reduce GHG emissions (Im et al., 2020).

For SM 2.1, the storage stage represents 36.19% of the total impacts of the global warming category, while in SM 3.1, the lagoons emit 12.44 kg CO2 eq or 38.56% of the total. For SM 5.1, the fertilization stage and the storage of digestate in the lagoons contribute with 15.78 1 0.62% of the global warming impact, in addition, fugitive emissions from the AD plant contribute 16.5% of the impact. SM 4.1 is the exception, where the most significant contribution to the global warming category is due to three reasons: (i) the transport of manure to the plant (e.g., 5.02 kgCO2eq); (ii) the CO2 emission from burning of fossil fuel (e.g., 4.93 kgCO2eq); (iii) and fugitive emissions from the AD plant (e.g., 8.29

found that biomethane use results in 27-50% lower emissions than natural gas in vehicles. For the authors, the CO2 released during the combustion of biomethane in the engine is from biogenic sources and has a closed cycle, considering that it can potentially be captured during the biomass growth phase, which shows promising potential for the use of biomethane as a substitute for fossil fuels.
61: Comparing scenarios as a function of the generated energy carrier (e. g. biomethane or electricity) is possible to verify that regardless of the energy carrier, they present a similar environmental performance with minor differences depending on project design and impact category (see Fig. 4).

In the global warming category, that shows a more significant reduction than the Baseline Scenario, the perspective of biomethane generation presents mitigation of emissions that varies from 74% to 114% compared to the Baseline Scenario, while scenarios considering electricity as the energy carrier present mitigation of emissions varying from 73% to 100%. The biomethane production is more favourable than the electricity generation, mainly for the scenarios SM 4 and SM 5, presenting mitigation, on average, 15% higher than the same scenarios adopting electricity generation. These differences occur mainly due to the avoided production and burning of diesel or gasoline, making sce- narios having biomethane as an energy carrier more environmentally
favourable due to replacing fossil fuel with a renewable source (Ardolino et al., 2018).

In a study by Gustafsson and Svensson (2021), the authors found that using biomethane from animal waste as a substitute for diesel signifi- cantly reduced impacts (e.g., 45-70%). By adding the contribution of the replacement of mineral fertilizers to the mitigation values from the replacement of fossil fuels, the impacts can be reduced by 100-125%, demonstrating the importance of animal waste management as a pre- cursor to changing our energy matrix (Sagastume Gutiérrez et al., 2020).

SM 4 and SM 5 scenarios (e.g., SM 4.1, SM 4.2, SM 5.1 and SM 5.2) present the best performance, and biomethane generation is more environmentally favourable for the stratospheric ozone depletion cate- gory in almost all scenarios. On average, stratospheric ozone depletion reduces 81.10 1 11.05% for the electricity generation scenarios and 81.56 + 10.41% for the biomethane generation scenarios, compared to the Baseline Scenario. As well as for the category ozone formation - terrestrial ecosystems, in which the biomethane generation presents the most significant mitigation potential compared to electricity generation, as much as 38 times less impact (in scenario 1.2) than in the baseline scenario.

For the terrestrial acidification category, the mitigations are very similar regardless of the energy carrier, ranging from 31.09 to 83.56%. For freshwater eutrophication, regardless of the energy carrier, all sce- narios cannot mitigate the impacts compared to the Baseline Scenario, with scenario 2 (2.1 and 2.2) has the worst environmental performance,

Terrestrial ecotoxicity|||||||| Freshwater eutrophication|||||||| Terrestrial acidification|||||||| Ozone formation, Terrestrial ecosystems|||||||| Stratospheric ozone depletion|||||||| Global warming||||||||
Fig. 4. Comparison of the results of the environmental impact assessment of the different scenarios compared to the baseline.

impacts about 427.32 + 10.78% higher. The same happens for terres- trial ecotoxicity due to the energy costs of the processes involved in the steps of AD, gas purification or conversion into electricity, and waste transportation when present (Duan et al., 2020).

Analyzing the configurations/scenarios studied, it is possible to verify that the characteristic of the waste/digestate generated in each biogas plant, in terms of concentration of nutrients and organic matter, is one of the factors that most influences the mitigation or generation of environmental impacts in the categories studied. For Pexas et al. (2020), the dilution of the digestate was one of the factors that impacted the reduction of emissions due to the reduction of effluent concentration. Besides the characteristic of the digestate, the transport of waste, either to the generation unit (e.g., SM 4) or inside the treatment plant for waste management - more specifically for the transport of material for com- posting in SM 2 and SM 3 scenarios - is one of the factors that most contributed to the negative results. Despite these impacts, the idea of biogas generation units with the waste being directed to an AD plant is an interesting alternative for the management of swine waste for the Brazilian production scenario, which has the baseline as the typical treatment for the swine chain waste (Brasil, 2016).

Although composting mitigates lagoon emissions due to the reduc- tion of organic matter present in the digestate, the need to apply solid compost to the crop employing agricultural tractors or transportation of waste to the composting unit, as well as the composting operation itself, have an increase in impacts in the categories analyzed. However, it is worth noting the importance of the recovery of nutrients contributing to the mitigation of impacts, and how composting enables the export of waste out of the production area, due to its solid characteristic, with less moisture, facilitating transport, which is extremely important consid- ering regions that have surplus production in relation to the soil absorptive capacity (Sarlaki et al., 2021).

The results show that using biomethane proves to be more favour- able than electricity generation. However, the authors recognize the limitations of their data, either due to: (i) the variability among the waste, which significantly affects the results (Jiang et al., 2021); (ii) the disregard of heat cogeneration in power generation plants, reducing environmental benefits of electricity generation; or (iii) factors inherent to the process, such as fugitive emissions, estimated in most cases, which increases the uncertainty of the results (Van den Oever et al., 2021). As a result, SM 4.2, composed of a centralized biodigestion unit operating with the input from neighboring production units, and SM 5.2, composed of distributed gas generation units and a central for gas collection and biomethane purification, has the best environmental performance compared to the other scenarios. Hence, it can also be characterized as a good alternative for managing gases generated in agricultural production units.

The concepts of AD plants are characterized as essential allies in the management of waste from Brazilian pig farming. However, in recent years the expansion of the sector has driven the construction of large industrial conglomerates. Many farms are still small, which can make the use of AD economically unviable (ABCS - Associação Brasileira dos Criadores de Suínos, 2016). According to a study conducted by Velás- quez Piñas et al. (2019), biogas plants with electricity production above 1000 kWe present the best economic performance, considering a mono-digestion plant the ideal size for the Brazilian scenario would be a 740 kWe biogas plant.

Given these results and considering the new Brazilian decarbon- ization policy, the case studies prove to be favourable to help achieve the atmospheric emission reduction targets, ratified by Brazil in the Paris agreement. The government committed itself to mitigate 37% of atmo- spheric emissions by 2025 and 43% by 2030 (Lima et al., 2020). The data from this study are preliminary, and it is necessary to extrapolate

the results to the other production chains, as well as to consider the production potential as a function of the national volume of effluents generated, considering the costs related to these processes, and the economic feasibility of these practices.

Despite these impacts, the idea of biogas generation units with the waste being directed to an AD plant is an interesting alternative for the management of swine waste for the Brazilian production scenario, which has the baseline as the typical treatment for the swine chain waste (Brasil, 2016).

Although composting mitigates lagoon emissions due to the reduc- tion of organic matter present in the digestate, the need to apply solid compost to the crop employing agricultural tractors or transportation of waste to the composting unit, as well as the composting operation itself, have an increase in impacts in the categories analyzed. However, it is worth noting the importance of the recovery of nutrients contributing to the mitigation of impacts, and how composting enables the export of waste out of the production area, due to its solid characteristic, with less moisture, facilitating transport, which is extremely important consid- ering regions that have surplus production in relation to the soil absorptive capacity (Sarlaki et al., 2021).

The results show that using biomethane proves to be more favour- able than electricity generation. However, the authors recognize the limitations of their data, either due to: (i) the variability among the waste, which significantly affects the results (Jiang et al., 2021); (ii) the disregard of heat cogeneration in power generation plants, reducing environmental benefits of electricity generation; or (iii) factors inherent to the process, such as fugitive emissions, estimated in most cases, which increases the uncertainty of the results (Van den Oever et al., 2021). As a result, SM 4.2, composed of a centralized biodigestion unit operating with the input from neighboring production units, and SM 5.2, composed of distributed gas generation units and a central for gas collection and biomethane purification, has the best environmental performance compared to the other scenarios. Hence, it can also be characterized as a good alternative for managing gases generated in agricultural production units.

The concepts of AD plants are characterized as essential allies in the management of waste from Brazilian pig farming. However, in recent years the expansion of the sector has driven the construction of large industrial conglomerates. Many farms are still small, which can make the use of AD economically unviable (ABCS - Associação Brasileira dos Criadores de Suínos, 2016). According to a study conducted by Velás- quez Piñas et al. (2019), biogas plants with electricity production above 1000 kWe present the best economic performance, considering a mono-digestion plant the ideal size for the Brazilian scenario would be a 740 kWe biogas plant.

Given these results and considering the new Brazilian decarbon- ization policy, the case studies prove to be favourable to help achieve the atmospheric emission reduction targets, ratified by Brazil in the Paris agreement. The government committed itself to mitigate 37% of atmo- spheric emissions by 2025 and 43% by 2030 (Lima et al., 2020). The data from this study are preliminary, and it is necessary to extrapolate

the results to the other production chains, as well as to consider the production potential as a function of the national volume of effluents generated, considering the costs related to these processes, and the economic feasibility of these practices.
66: Comparing scenarios as a function of the generated energy carrier (e. g. biomethane or electricity) is possible to verify that regardless of the energy carrier, they present a similar environmental performance with minor differences depending on project design and impact category (see Fig. 4).

In the global warming category, that shows a more significant reduction than the Baseline Scenario, the perspective of biomethane generation presents mitigation of emissions that varies from 74% to 114% compared to the Baseline Scenario, while scenarios considering electricity as the energy carrier present mitigation of emissions varying from 73% to 100%. The biomethane production is more favourable than the electricity generation, mainly for the scenarios SM 4 and SM 5, presenting mitigation, on average, 15% higher than the same scenarios adopting electricity generation. These differences occur mainly due to the avoided production and burning of diesel or gasoline, making sce- narios having biomethane as an energy carrier more environmentally
favourable due to replacing fossil fuel with a renewable source (Ardolino et al., 2018).

In a study by Gustafsson and Svensson (2021), the authors found that using biomethane from animal waste as a substitute for diesel signifi- cantly reduced impacts (e.g., 45-70%). By adding the contribution of the replacement of mineral fertilizers to the mitigation values from the replacement of fossil fuels, the impacts can be reduced by 100-125%, demonstrating the importance of animal waste management as a pre- cursor to changing our energy matrix (Sagastume Gutiérrez et al., 2020).

SM 4 and SM 5 scenarios (e.g., SM 4.1, SM 4.2, SM 5.1 and SM 5.2) present the best performance, and biomethane generation is more environmentally favourable for the stratospheric ozone depletion cate- gory in almost all scenarios. On average, stratospheric ozone depletion reduces 81.10 1 11.05% for the electricity generation scenarios and 81.56 + 10.41% for the biomethane generation scenarios, compared to the Baseline Scenario. As well as for the category ozone formation - terrestrial ecosystems, in which the biomethane generation presents the most significant mitigation potential compared to electricity generation, as much as 38 times less impact (in scenario 1.2) than in the baseline scenario.

For the terrestrial acidification category, the mitigations are very similar regardless of the energy carrier, ranging from 31.09 to 83.56%. For freshwater eutrophication, regardless of the energy carrier, all sce- narios cannot mitigate the impacts compared to the Baseline Scenario, with scenario 2 (2.1 and 2.2) has the worst environmental performance,

70: The use of energy from swine waste through AD proved to be an environmentally favourable technology, since both electricity produc- tion and biomethane reduced the environmental impacts associated with the waste treatment steps. However, in terms of reducing the global warming potential, the generation of biomethane proved to be more efficient by mitigating up to 114% of GHG emissions, which can contribute to the developing countries programs and achieving GHG reduction targets, along with efficient waste management. In addition, it was possible to verify that the systems configured as biogas plants are a crucial component for the energy transition and decarbonization of the Brazilian energy matrix. However, economic analyses and extrapolation of the data considering the energy potential of swine manure on a na- tional level should be performed in future works, to ensure the feasibility of the results found in the economic sphere. Finally, this study provides valuable information for the sustainable future of the Brazilian swine meat chain and how different forms of energy use can promote the decarbonization of the sector.
